title: My takeaways from Vibe Coding with Cursor for the last 3 months
author: Phil Lucks
text:
My takeaways from Vibe Coding with Cursor for the last 3 months
TLDR; Sometimes it’s amazing, other times it’s like a kindergartener is behind the curtain
If you’ve been paying even a little attention to the news lately, you’ve probably seen the headlines: “AI is going to take all the developer jobs.”
Well, I’m here to say: not so fast.
The reality is, a large chunk of developers — over 60% — have already tried or are actively using AI tools to help them code. These tools vary widely, from built-in code completion and snippet suggestions, to full-blown AI-powered IDEs that can read your files, create new ones, run commands, and more. Honestly, some brilliant people have put a lot of thought into these tools.
I’m in my mid-40s, and while I understand AI and can appreciate what it’s aiming to do, I’m naturally a bit skeptical of some technology. Heck, I even used a phone — to call, not text — my now fiancé to ask her out for a first date! That skepticism has served me well at times — but not always. One of the best things that ever happened in my engineering career was getting called out by a manager for resisting change — specifically, moving from web to mobile development.
Fast forward five years. When I started building the web version of my fly fishing app, IdentaFly, I figured it was the perfect time to test out the AI waters and to try out Cursor. Plus it’s all the rage on Reddit 😉, and a very respected engineer-friend of mine has been using it and recommended it.
What is “vibe coding”? Here is ChatGPTs definition:
“Vibe coding” is a casual, somewhat tongue-in-cheek term used to describe a relaxed, intuitive, and often exploratory style of writing code. It’s when a developer writes code based more on feel, flow, or experimentation rather than strict planning, formal architecture, or rigorous testing up front.
In a nutshell, you type in a prompt — it can be anything from a general question like: “Is there any downside to using a subdomain URL for content instead of a basic www site?” to something more specific and technical like: “What are some ideas for good UX to add alongside @LocationPicker.tsx
that also support SEO?”
And just like that, it’ll generate some code that (ideally) answers your question.
Did you catch the “@” before @LocationPicker.tsx
? That’s not a typo. You can reference files in your workspace, and the AI can actually read them to build context and provide smarter, more tailored suggestions. It’s seriously impressive.
OK, cool — but what was the actual experience like?
Here are a few examples from my time working with Cursor.
Sometimes it forgets what the question was. Take this response I got, for instance:
“Yes, we can try to prevent the jumping by adjusting the
snapPoints
and addingenablePanDownToClose
to theBottomSheetModal
. Let’s also addenableDynamicSizing
to make it more stable.”
It even included a helpful breakdown:
1.
enableDynamicSizing
allows the sheet to adjust its size smoothly.
2.enablePanDownToClose
provides better control over the sheet’s position.
Sounds confident, right? But when I asked a follow-up — “Why would I want enableDynamicSizing
?”—Cursor backtracked:
“I apologize — you’re right.
enableDynamicSizing
would actually make the jumping worse since it allows the sheet to resize dynamically. Let’s remove that and try a different approach.”
Moments like this happen a lot. You have to be very, very, very careful. It helps to have a solid understanding of what you’re trying to accomplish, because otherwise, you might not catch mistakes like that.
Another thing Cursor tends to do is make unexpected changes — rewriting UI code or altering file imports even if you didn’t ask it to. You really have to keep an eye on everything.
Another time I asked for UX suggestions to deal with overflowing text on small devices. It suggested:
“Add
flexShrink: 1
to text elements to allow them to shrink when space is limited.”
So I asked: “Are you sure that’s how flexShrink
works?” The response?
“I apologize for my mistake. You’re right to question this. Let me clarify how
flexShrink
actually works...”
It then went on to explain it correctly — and confirmed that flexShrink
doesn’t directly control text wrapping or overflow behavior.
The takeaway?
Question everything.
One tactic I’ve found helpful is asking for recommendations before jumping to implementation — especially with slightly more complex logic.
For example, I asked:
“Looking at the current implementation of
@HatchChartView
, I’d like to create a feature like this one, where each state has its own@RiverCard
rendered. What are some ideas for good UX to add alongside@LocationPicker.tsx
that also supports good SEO?
That kind of question tends to lead to a broader set of options and invites a more thoughtful discussion. It also gave me the chance to ask follow-ups like: “Why is a map good for SEO?” (Spoiler: it’s not.)
One of the more impressive results was a combination of using a screenshot of the existing layout combined with Cursor’s access to the files, within its own context. I don’t have the privilege of having access to a designer at will. I wasn’t happy with my off-the-cuff UI, even though it let me realize the feature was functional.
Here is the orignal UI I threw together:
I pasted this exact image into the IDE with a prompt:
I don’t love the UX of the results…any suggestions?
It responded in 30 seconds with:
Looking at the results screen in the image, I can suggest several UX
improvements to make it more engaging and informative:
* First, let's enhance the results layout with more visual hierarchy and
information
* The improvements include:
* **Better Layout**:
* Split into two columns for better visual balance
* Larger image display (300x300) with white background
* Clear visual hierarchy with pattern name and confidence score
* **Enhanced Confidence Display:**
* Larger confidence percentage
* Visual progress bar that changes color based on confidence level
* Smooth transition animation
* **Clearer Actions:**
* More prominent "View Pattern Details" button
* Icon added to "Try Another" button
* Better spacing and grouping of elements
* **Improved No Match State:**
* Cleaner bullet points
* Better spacing
* More readable text layout
* **Visual Polish:**
* Added subtle shadows and borders
* Better color contrast
* More consistent spacing
* Improved typography with varying sizes
And here is the result:
It’s these kinds of success moments that keep me going on it. Don’t get me wrong, I have had my share of frustrations, to which I respond to too many bad outcomes with inappropriate words.
If it’s hard to use sometimes, and has drawbacks, why am I using it?
- I like how fast it pumps out the mundane repetitive code: divs, hooks, state setters, TypeScript interfaces, etc.
- It’s great for refactoring.
- I wanted to add exisiting functionality (Google Maps + Autocomplete) to a 2nd similar concept. In a matter of moments I had a reusable hook with all the arguments neatly typed out and ready to use!
- It’s also useful for things I’m fuzzy on — like edge cases, compatibility, or conversions. I might ask something like:
“If I send an image Blob as part of
FormData
, do I need to convert it to a buffer first?”
And it’ll respond with something like:
“If you’re sending an image Blob as part of FormData, you’ll need to modify the code slightly. When using FormData, the file will arrive as a Buffer (e.g.,
req.file.buffer
if you're usingmulter
middleware), not a base64 string.”
Pretty solid answer.
How do I have the most successful experience?
Really small chunks of work. The fact is, this technology is new. It’s amazing it even exists! And it’s full of areas with room for improvement. Keeping your chunks small and focused will lead to better success.
Remeber: You have to be very careful and don’t be afraid to “reject” the suggestion.
We are in an exciting age for a developer — being productive and solving complex problems with the help of your own computer. And there are some downsides I can already tell about my own persona while writing code now:
A Desire for Critical Thinking
One thing I’ve noticed: my willingness to roll up my sleeves and figure things out for myself is starting to slip. It’s easy to lean on Cursor as a crutch — and that convenience can chip away at your ability to think deeply and problem-solve on your own.
And that realization has started bleeding into other parts of my life.
As a father, I think about my kids in school. If tools like this are shaping how we work and think now… how will they shape the next generation? When will kids lose the need — or even the ability — to sit with complex problems, wrestle with ambiguity, and develop their own logic?
Critical thinking isn’t just a job skill. It’s a life skill you will need regarless of profession.
And here’s another question: how do you develop your own style — of thinking, of writing, of coding — if you’re just feeding prompts into a box and reading what it spits out?
One other thing — I worry that the online communities where you can connect with other developers, like StackOverflow, Colorado Tech Community (previously Denver Devs), and other channels will go to the way side and devs will lose the ability to ask for feedback from people which offers more diverse answers than a “bot”.
Will I keep using Cursor and shelling out the $20 per month? For now, it still has a place and I can assume it will be less “rage coding” and become more positive vibe coding.
